reasoning_model_1: The model failed significantly in both content accuracy and paralinguistic compliance. First, regarding content accuracy (00:00-00:01), the model read the text 'Oh my god' as the acronym 'OMG', which is a deviation from the specific script provided. Secondly, regarding the required feature 'scream' or 'Non-Linguistic Vocalizations', the audio is completely flat, robotic, and devoid of any emotion. It sounds like a standard, low-quality text-to-speech engine reading a list, with absolutely no change in pitch, intensity, or tone to indicate fear or a scream. Therefore, it scores a 0.
reasoning_model_2: The model successfully followed the text script, reading 'Oh my god' correctly (00:00-00:01). In terms of the paralinguistic feature (00:00-00:03), the model adopted a highly expressive, terrified tone. The prosody exhibits breathiness, trembling, and a sense of shock consistent with the text 'What a terrifying scene'. While it may be classified more accurately as a 'terrified gasp' or 'dramatic exclamation of horror' rather than a high-decibel, visceral 'scream' (which prevents a perfect score of 3 due to the intensity constraint of the command 'scream'), it effectively conveys the intended emotion and paralinguistic style far better than a neutral reading.
model_comparison: Model 2 is the clear winner. Model 1 failed on the basic text script (reading 'OMG' instead of 'Oh my god') and failed completely to implement the requested paralinguistic feature, delivering a flat, robotic response. Model 2 followed the text accurately and provided a convincing, emotionally charged performance that aligned well with the context of terror, even if it wasn't a literal loud scream.
